Jab aap ek LLM use karte ho, jab neural network train hota hai, jab RAG system embedding dhundta hai — har jagah yahi mathematics kaam karti hai. Bina iske samjhe aap ek parrot ki tarah AI use karenge. Iske saath samajhkar aap ek AI Engineer banenge.

📊 Chapter Progress Tracker

CHAPTER 01 • PART 2

📐 Linear Algebra

AI ka backbone — data ko numbers mein samajhna, transform karna, aur represent karna

🌟Introduction — Linear Algebra Kyun?

Socho ek baar — ChatGPT ke paas aaya tumhara question: "Mujhe Python seekhni hai." ChatGPT ne tumhara jawab diya. Lekin andar kya hua? Tumhara text pehle numbers mein convert hua — vectors mein. Phir in vectors pe matrix operations hue. Phir neural network ne in numbers ko process kiya, aur output wapis text mein aaya.

Yahi hai Linear Algebra ka role AI mein. Har image ek matrix hai. Har word ek vector hai. Har neural network layer ek matrix multiplication hai. Linear Algebra ke bina AI exist hi nahi karta.

Real-World Analogy — Address System ki Tarah
Jaise GPS coordinates ek jagah ko numbers se represent karte hain (28.6° N, 77.2° E = Delhi), waise hi Linear Algebra har cheez ko numbers ki language mein represent karta hai. Machine ko numbers samajh aate hain, text nahi. Linear Algebra woh translator hai jo duniya ko numbers mein convert karta hai.
1.1 Scalars — Ek Akela Number
Scalar kya hota hai?

Scalar ek single number hota hai — sirf magnitude hoti hai, koi direction nahi. Jaise temperature, age, price — ye sab scalars hain.

AI mein scalars bahut jagah aate hain — learning rate ek scalar hai (jaise 0.001), loss value ek scalar hai (jaise 0.342), temperature parameter LLMs mein ek scalar hai.

Python
# Scalar examples in Python
import numpy as np

# Simple scalars
temperature = 98.6          # Body temperature
learning_rate = 0.001       # AI training mein use hota hai
loss = 0.342               # Model ka error value
llm_temperature = 0.7      # ChatGPT ka creativity parameter

# NumPy scalar
a = np.float32(3.14)
print(type(a))             # numpy.float32
print(a.shape)             # () — koi dimension nahi

# Scalar operations
result = learning_rate * 10 + 0.5
print(f"Result: {result}")   # Result: 0.51
📤 Output
<class 'numpy.float32'> () Result: 0.510...
1.2 Vectors — Direction Wale Numbers
Vector kya hota hai?

Vector numbers ki ek ordered list hoti hai — magnitude bhi hoti hai aur direction bhi. Ise ek arrow ki tarah socho jo space mein kisi jagah point karta hai.

AI mein vectors bohot fundamental hain:

  • Word Embedding: "King" word ko [0.2, -0.5, 0.8, 0.1, ...] jaise 512-dimensional vector se represent karte hain
  • Image pixels: Ek row of pixels ek vector hai
  • User preferences: Recommendation systems mein user ek vector hota hai
  • Neural network neurons: Har layer ka output ek vector hota hai
Vector — 2D aur 3D Representation
Python — Vectors
import numpy as np

# ===== Vector Creation =====
v1 = np.array([3, 4])           # 2D vector
v2 = np.array([1, 2, 3])         # 3D vector
word_vec = np.random.randn(512)   # 512D word embedding jaise

# ===== Basic Properties =====
print("Shape:", v1.shape)        # (2,)
print("Dtype:", v1.dtype)        # int64
print("Size:", v1.size)          # 2

# ===== Vector Operations =====
a = np.array([1, 2, 3])
b = np.array([4, 5, 6])

# Addition — vectors add up component-wise
print("a + b =", a + b)           # [5, 7, 9]

# Scalar multiplication
print("2 * a =", 2 * a)           # [2, 4, 6]

# Magnitude (length) of vector
magnitude = np.linalg.norm(v1)
print(f"v1 ka magnitude = {magnitude}") # 5.0 (3-4-5 triangle)

# Unit vector (direction only, magnitude=1)
unit_v = v1 / np.linalg.norm(v1)
print("Unit vector:", unit_v)      # [0.6, 0.8]

# ===== AI Use Case: Cosine Similarity =====
# Kitna similar hain do vectors? (0 = different, 1 = same)
def cosine_similarity(v1, v2):
    dot = np.dot(v1, v2)
    mag = np.linalg.norm(v1) * np.linalg.norm(v2)
    return dot / mag

king  = np.array([0.9, 0.1, 0.8])  # "king" embedding
queen = np.array([0.8, 0.2, 0.7])  # "queen" embedding
car   = np.array([0.1, 0.9, 0.2])  # "car" embedding

print(f"King-Queen similarity: {cosine_similarity(king, queen):.3f}")
print(f"King-Car similarity:   {cosine_similarity(king, car):.3f}")
📤 Output
Shape: (2,) Dtype: int64 Size: 2 a + b = [5 7 9] 2 * a = [2 4 6] v1 ka magnitude = 5.0 Unit vector: [0.6 0.8] King-Queen similarity: 0.997 ← bohot similar! King-Car similarity: 0.573 ← alag hain
💡 AI Insight
RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) systems mein exactly yahi cosine similarity use hoti hai! Jab tum koi question poochte ho, uska embedding vector banta hai, phir database mein sabse similar vectors dhundhe jaate hain. Ye hi "relevant documents" hote hain jo LLM ko context ke liye diye jaate hain.
1.3 Matrices — 2D Grid of Numbers
Matrix kya hai?

Matrix numbers ka ek 2D grid hota hai — rows aur columns mein arrange kiya hua. Ise vectors ki list ki tarah bhi socha ja sakta hai.

AI mein matrices har jagah hain:

  • Images: Ek 28×28 grayscale image = 28×28 matrix
  • Weight matrices: Neural networks ke connections
  • Attention matrices: Transformer mein query-key attention scores
  • Embeddings lookup table: Vocabulary size × embedding dimension
Matrix — AI mein Kahan Use Hoti Hai
Python — Matrices
import numpy as np

# ===== Matrix Creation =====
A = np.array([
    [1, 2, 3],   # Row 1
    [4, 5, 6],   # Row 2
    [7, 8, 9]    # Row 3
])  # Shape: (3, 3)

print("Matrix A:\n", A)
print("Shape:", A.shape)     # (3, 3)
print("Rows:", A.shape[0])   # 3
print("Cols:", A.shape[1])   # 3

# ===== Special Matrices =====
zeros    = np.zeros((3, 3))    # All zeros
ones     = np.ones((3, 3))     # All ones
identity = np.eye(3)           # Identity matrix
random_m = np.random.randn(3, 3) # Random (weight init)

print("\nIdentity Matrix:\n", identity)

# ===== Element Access =====
print("\nElement [1][2]:", A[1][2])  # 6 (row 1, col 2)
print("Row 0:", A[0])               # [1 2 3]
print("Col 0:", A[:, 0])            # [1 4 7]

# ===== Transpose (rows ↔ columns) =====
B = np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]])  # Shape (2, 3)
print("\nB.T (Transpose):\n", B.T)     # Shape (3, 2)
print("B shape:", B.shape,           # (2, 3)
      "  B.T shape:", B.T.shape)     # (3, 2)
1.4 Dot Product — Similarity ka Measure
Dot Product kya hai?

Do vectors ka dot product ek single number deta hai jo batata hai ki woh kitne ek direction mein hain. Formula: a·b = Σ(aᵢ × bᵢ)

Dot Product Formula
a · b = a₁b₁ + a₂b₂ + a₃b₃ + ... + aₙbₙ
= Σᵢ aᵢ bᵢ
Agar a = [1, 2, 3] aur b = [4, 5, 6] toh:
a · b = (1×4) + (2×5) + (3×6) = 4 + 10 + 18 = 32

Dot product AI mein sabse fundamental operation hai:

  • Neural network neurons: Har neuron inputs ka dot product + bias calculate karta hai
  • Attention mechanism: Query aur Key vectors ka dot product = attention score
  • Similarity search: Vectors kitne similar hain = dot product (normalized = cosine similarity)
1.5 Matrix Multiplication — AI ka Core Operation

Matrix multiplication AI mein sabse important operation hai. Jab bhi neural network forward pass karta hai — woh matrix multiplication kar raha hota hai.

Matrix Multiplication Rule
C = A × B
A ki shape (m × k) honi chahiye, B ki (k × n) — result C ki shape (m × n) hogi.
C[i][j] = Σₖ A[i][k] × B[k][j] — Row of A dot Column of B
Matrix Multiplication — Visual Step-by-Step
Python — Matrix Multiplication
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[1, 2, 3],
               [4, 5, 6]])   # Shape: (2, 3)

B = np.array([[7,  8],
               [9,  10],
               [11, 12]])     # Shape: (3, 2)

# Method 1: np.matmul
C = np.matmul(A, B)
print("C =\n", C)              # [[58, 64], [139, 154]]
print("Shape:", C.shape)       # (2, 2)

# Method 2: @ operator (Python 3.5+)
C2 = A @ B
print("\nC2 =\n", C2)           # Same result

# ===== Neural Network Forward Pass Simulation =====
# Input: batch of 32 samples, 784 features (MNIST flattened)
X = np.random.randn(32, 784)  # (32, 784) - 32 images

# Weight matrix: 784 inputs → 256 hidden neurons
W1 = np.random.randn(784, 256) * 0.01
b1 = np.zeros(256)

# Forward pass: Hidden layer output
H = X @ W1 + b1    # (32, 784) @ (784, 256) = (32, 256)
print(f"\nHidden layer output shape: {H.shape}")

# Second layer: 256 → 10 (10 classes)
W2 = np.random.randn(256, 10) * 0.01
output = H @ W2    # (32, 256) @ (256, 10) = (32, 10)
print(f"Output shape: {output.shape}")
# Shape batata hai: 32 images, har ek ke liye 10 class scores
📤 Output
C = [[ 58 64] [139 154]] Shape: (2, 2) Hidden layer output shape: (32, 256) Output shape: (32, 10)
1.6 Tensors — Generalized Arrays
Tensor kya hai?

Tensor numbers ka generalized container hai. Scalar = 0D tensor, Vector = 1D tensor, Matrix = 2D tensor, phir 3D, 4D, 5D... sab tensors hain. PyTorch aur TensorFlow sab tensors pe kaam karte hain.

DimensionNameShape ExampleAI Use Case
0DScalar()Loss value, learning rate
1DVector(512,)Word embedding, hidden state
2DMatrix(batch, features)Input data, weight matrix
3DTensor(batch, seq, features)NLP sequences, time series
4DTensor(batch, H, W, C)Batch of images (CNN)
5DTensor(batch, frames, H, W, C)Video data
Python — Tensors with PyTorch
import torch

# ===== Creating Tensors =====
scalar   = torch.tensor(3.14)        # 0D
vector   = torch.tensor([1, 2, 3])     # 1D
matrix   = torch.randn(3, 4)          # 2D
image    = torch.randn(3, 224, 224)   # 3D (C, H, W)
batch    = torch.randn(32, 3, 224, 224)# 4D batch

print(f"Scalar dim: {scalar.dim()}, shape: {scalar.shape}")
print(f"Vector dim: {vector.dim()}, shape: {vector.shape}")
print(f"Image  dim: {image.dim()},  shape: {image.shape}")
print(f"Batch  dim: {batch.dim()},  shape: {batch.shape}")

# ===== NLP Tensor Example =====
batch_size  = 32    # 32 sentences at once
seq_len     = 128   # 128 tokens per sentence
embed_dim   = 768   # BERT-style embedding size

nlp_tensor = torch.randn(batch_size, seq_len, embed_dim)
print(f"\nNLP Tensor shape: {nlp_tensor.shape}")
# torch.Size([32, 128, 768])

# ===== GPU Transfer =====
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    t = nlp_tensor.cuda()   # GPU pe move karo
    print(f"Device: {t.device}")  # cuda:0
1.7 Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors — Data ka DNA
Eigenvalue/Eigenvector kya hai?

Ek matrix A ke liye, agar koi vector v hai aur ek number λ (lambda) hai jaise ki: Av = λv — toh v ek eigenvector hai aur λ ek eigenvalue hai. Matrix sirf vector ko scale karta hai, rotate nahi karta.

Eigenvalue Equation
A × v = λ × v
A = matrix | v = eigenvector | λ (lambda) = eigenvalue
Iska matlab: Matrix A, vector v ko sirf λ times scale karta hai — direction same rehti hai!

AI mein eigenvalues aur eigenvectors ka use:

  • PCA (Principal Component Analysis): Data ke most important directions dhundhne ke liye — dimensionality reduction
  • Google PageRank: Web pages ki ranking ke liye eigenvector use hota tha
  • Stability Analysis: Neural networks ki training stability check karne ke liye
  • Spectral Clustering: Graph-based ML algorithms mein
Python — Eigenvalues & PCA
import numpy as np
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ===== Eigenvalue Calculation =====
A = np.array([[3, 1],
               [1, 3]])

eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(A)
print("Eigenvalues:", eigenvalues)       # [4. 2.]
print("Eigenvectors:\n", eigenvectors)   # columns are eigenvectors

# Verify: Av = λv
v = eigenvectors[:, 0]   # First eigenvector
λ = eigenvalues[0]       # First eigenvalue
print("\nAv:", A @ v)
print("λv:", λ * v)         # Same! ✓

# ===== PCA — Real AI Use Case =====
# 1000 data points with 10 features → reduce to 2
np.random.seed(42)
data = np.random.randn(1000, 10)  # 1000 samples, 10 features

pca = PCA(n_components=2)
reduced = pca.fit_transform(data)

print(f"\nOriginal shape: {data.shape}")    # (1000, 10)
print(f"Reduced shape:  {reduced.shape}")  # (1000, 2)
print(f"Variance explained: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_}")
# Pehle 2 components kitna variance explain karte hain

🎯 Linear Algebra Interview Questions

Q1. "Cosine similarity aur Euclidean distance mein kya difference hai? RAG mein kaunsa use karte hain aur kyun?"
Q2. "Matrix multiplication ki shape rules explain karo. (2,3) @ (3,4) = ? aur (3,4) @ (2,3) kyun nahi hoga?"
Q3. "PCA kya hai aur yeh eigenvalues se kaise related hai? LLMs mein dimensionality reduction kab use karte hain?"
Q4. "Ek transformer mein attention score kaise calculate hota hai? Q, K, V matrices kya hain?"
Q5. "Transpose operation ka neural networks mein kya role hai? Backpropagation mein kaise use hota hai?"

🖊 Linear Algebra Practice Exercises

  1. NumPy use karke do 3D vectors ka dot product calculate karo aur cosine similarity find karo.
  2. Ek (4,3) aur (3,2) matrix multiply karo manually, phir NumPy se verify karo.
  3. Apne naam ke har letter ki ASCII value nikalo, unka ek vector banao, aur uska magnitude calculate karo.
  4. 50 random 2D points generate karo, PCA se 1D reduce karo, aur visualize karo ki kaunsa component sabse zyada variance explain karta hai.
  5. Word2Vec-style: "King - Man + Woman" operation vector arithmetic se try karo (random vectors se).

📌 Linear Algebra — Key Takeaways

  • Scalar: Single number — learning rate, temperature, loss value
  • Vector: Ordered numbers — word embeddings, hidden states, user preferences
  • Matrix: 2D grid — weight matrices, image data, attention scores
  • Tensor: N-dimensional — PyTorch/TensorFlow ka base data type
  • Dot Product: Similarity measure — attention mechanism ka core
  • Matrix Multiply: Neural network forward pass ka foundation
  • Eigenvalues/PCA: Data compression aur feature extraction
  • Cosine Similarity: RAG systems mein document retrieval
CHAPTER 02 • PART 2

🎲 Probability & Statistics

Uncertainty ko measure karna — AI ke decisions ka foundation

🌟Introduction — Probability Kyun Zaroori Hai?

AI koi crystal ball nahi hai — woh certainties nahi, probabilities deal karta hai. Jab ChatGPT koi word predict karta hai, woh ek probability distribution se sample karta hai. Jab spam filter email ko spam classify karta hai, woh probability calculate karta hai. Jab recommendation system Netflix pe movie suggest karta hai, woh probability of user liking predict karta hai.

Real-World Analogy — Weather Forecast ki Tarah
"Kal 70% chance of rain" — yeh ek probability statement hai. AI hamesha aisa sochta hai. "Is email ke spam hone ka 93% chance hai." "Is patient ke diabetes hone ka 67% probability hai." "Next word 'the' hone ki 34% probability, 'a' ki 28%..." — Probability ek AI ki natural language hai!
2.1 Mean — Average Value
Mean (Arithmetic Mean) kya hai?

Saare values ka sum, total count se divide karna. Yeh central tendency ka sabse common measure hai — "typical value" batata hai.

Mean Formula
μ = (1/N) × Σᵢ xᵢ = (x₁ + x₂ + ... + xₙ) / N
μ (mu) = population mean | N = total count | xᵢ = individual values
Example: [2, 4, 6, 8, 10] → Mean = (2+4+6+8+10)/5 = 30/5 = 6

AI mein mean kahan use hota hai:

  • Batch Normalization: Neural networks mein har layer ke outputs ka mean subtract karte hain
  • Mean Squared Error (MSE): Most common loss function
  • Feature scaling: Data preprocessing mein mean use karte hain
  • Ensemble methods: Multiple models ke predictions ka mean = final prediction
2.2 Median — Middle Value
Median kya hai?

Data ko sort karne ke baad beech wali value. Yeh outliers se affected nahi hoti — isliye median mean se zyada "robust" hoti hai.

Analogy — Salary Example
Ek company mein 9 employees hain jिनकी salary hai: [20K, 25K, 30K, 32K, 35K, 40K, 45K, 50K, 500K].
Mean = ~86K (CEO ki 500K salary se distorted!) | Median = 35K (actually representative!)
Isliye median zyada "fair" hota hai jab outliers ho.
2.3 Mode — Sabse Common Value
Mode kya hai?

Jo value sabse zyada baar repeat hoti hai. Categorical data ke liye best central tendency measure. Ek dataset bimodal ya multimodal bhi ho sakta hai.

2.4 Variance — Spread of Data
Variance kya hai?

Data kitna spread out hai mean se — yeh variability ka measure hai. High variance = data bohot scattered. Low variance = data mean ke paas.

Variance Formula
σ² = (1/N) Σᵢ (xᵢ − μ)²
σ² (sigma squared) = variance | μ = mean | N = count
Har value ko mean se subtract karo, square karo, phir sab ka average lo.
Squaring kyun? Negative differences positive ho jaate hain aur bade differences zyada penalize hote hain.
2.5 Standard Deviation — Variance ka Square Root
Standard Deviation
σ = √σ² = √[(1/N) Σᵢ (xᵢ − μ)²]
σ (sigma) = standard deviation | Same unit mein hoti hai data ke
Variance: squared units mein (e.g., meters²) | Std Dev: same units (meters)
Isliye std dev zyada interpretable hai!
Mean, Variance, Std Dev — Visual Comparison
Python — Statistics Fundamentals
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# ===== Sample Dataset: AI model evaluation scores =====
scores = np.array([72, 85, 78, 92, 68, 88, 75, 95, 71, 83])

# ===== Measures of Central Tendency =====
mean   = np.mean(scores)
median = np.median(scores)
mode   = stats.mode(scores)

print(f"Mean   (Average):   {mean:.2f}")     # 80.70
print(f"Median (Middle):    {median:.2f}")   # 81.50
print(f"Mode   (Frequent):  {mode.mode[0]}") # No repeat = each appears once

# ===== Measures of Spread =====
variance = np.var(scores)
std_dev  = np.std(scores)
data_range = np.max(scores) - np.min(scores)

print(f"\nVariance (σ²):      {variance:.2f}")
print(f"Std Dev  (σ):       {std_dev:.2f}")
print(f"Range:              {data_range}")
print(f"Min: {np.min(scores)}, Max: {np.max(scores)}")

# ===== AI Context: Evaluating Model Performance =====
model_a_scores = np.array([80, 81, 79, 82, 80])  # Consistent model
model_b_scores = np.array([90, 60, 85, 55, 95])  # Inconsistent model

print(f"\nModel A — Mean: {np.mean(model_a_scores):.1f}, Std: {np.std(model_a_scores):.1f}")
print(f"Model B — Mean: {np.mean(model_b_scores):.1f}, Std: {np.std(model_b_scores):.1f}")
# Model A low std dev → reliable! Model B high std dev → unreliable!

# ===== Z-Score: Standardization =====
# AI mein input features normalize karte hain
z_scores = (scores - np.mean(scores)) / np.std(scores)
print(f"\nZ-scores: {z_scores.round(2)}")
# Positive = above mean, Negative = below mean
📤 Output
Mean (Average): 80.70 Median (Middle): 81.50 Mode (Frequent): 68 Variance (σ²): 71.21 Std Dev (σ): 8.44 Range: 27 Min: 68, Max: 95 Model A — Mean: 80.0, Std: 0.9 ← Bahut reliable! Model B — Mean: 77.0, Std: 16.7 ← Unreliable! Z-scores: [-1.03 0.51 -0.32 1.33 -1.51 0.87 -0.68 1.69 -1.15 0.27]
2.6 Probability Distributions — Data Patterns

Probability distribution batata hai ki kaunsa value kitna likely hai. Different types of data different distributions follow karte hain.

Normal Distribution

Bell curve. Heights, weights, IQ scores, measurement errors. AI mein weight initialization, batch normalization.

Bernoulli Distribution

Ek trial, do outcomes (0 or 1). Coin flip. Binary classification ka foundation.

Binomial Distribution

N trials mein k successes. Spam detection mein kitni emails spam. Multiple binary events.

Uniform Distribution

Sab values equally likely. Dice roll. Random number generation, random initialization.

Softmax Distribution

LLM ka output distribution — next token probability. Sab probabilities sum = 1.

Normal Distribution — Bell Curve (AI mein Sabse Important)
2.7 Conditional Probability — "Given That" ki Probability
Conditional Probability kya hai?

P(A|B) = "B hone ki condition mein A ki probability". Event B already ho chuka hai — iske baad A kitna likely hai? Yeh AI mein bayesian thinking ka base hai.

Conditional Probability Formula
P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B)
P(A|B) = A ki probability given B | P(A ∩ B) = dono saath hone ki probability
Example: P(Spam | "Free Money") = P(Spam AND "Free Money") / P("Free Money")
Agar "Free Money" wali 90% emails spam hain → P(Spam|"Free Money") = 0.90
2.8 Bayes' Theorem — AI ka Core Foundation
Bayes' Theorem kya hai?

Evidence ke basis pe beliefs update karna. Yeh Naive Bayes classifier, Bayesian neural networks, spam filters, aur kaafi AI algorithms ka foundation hai.

Bayes' Theorem
P(A|B) = [P(B|A) × P(A)] / P(B)
Posterior: P(A|B) — Evidence B dekhne ke baad A ki updated probability
Likelihood: P(B|A) — Agar A true hota toh B ka kitna chance tha
Prior: P(A) — Evidence se pehle A ki probability
Evidence: P(B) — B ki overall probability (normalization constant)
Step-by-Step Bayes Example — Medical Diagnosis
Situation: Ek disease 1% population mein hoti hai. Test 95% accurate hai.

Tum test positive aaye. Kya tum actually beemar ho?

• P(Disease) = 0.01 (1% population mein hoti hai) ← Prior
• P(Positive | Disease) = 0.95 (95% accurate test) ← Likelihood
• P(Positive | No Disease) = 0.05 (5% false positive)
• P(Positive) = (0.95×0.01) + (0.05×0.99) = 0.0095 + 0.0495 = 0.059

P(Disease | Positive) = (0.95 × 0.01) / 0.059 ≈ 0.161 = 16.1%!

Surprise! Even with 95% accurate test, agar disease rare hai toh positive test bhi sirf 16% chance deta hai! Yahi Bayes ka power hai — prior probability matter karta hai!
Python — Bayes' Theorem & Naive Bayes
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
import numpy as np

# ===== Manual Bayes Calculation =====
# Email spam detection example

# Prior probabilities
P_spam    = 0.30   # 30% emails spam hain
P_not_spam = 0.70  # 70% emails legitimate hain

# Likelihood: "FREE" word ka probability
P_free_given_spam    = 0.80  # Spam mein "FREE" 80% baar
P_free_given_not_spam = 0.05 # Normal email mein "FREE" 5%

# Evidence: P("FREE")
P_free = (P_free_given_spam * P_spam +
           P_free_given_not_spam * P_not_spam)

# Posterior: P(Spam | "FREE") = Bayes Theorem
P_spam_given_free = (P_free_given_spam * P_spam) / P_free

print(f"P(FREE): {P_free:.3f}")
print(f"P(Spam | FREE word): {P_spam_given_free:.3f} = {P_spam_given_free*100:.1f}%")

# ===== Naive Bayes Spam Classifier =====
# Training data
emails = [
    "free money click here win prize",      # spam
    "dear friend meeting tomorrow office",   # ham
    "win lottery free cash urgent",           # spam
    "project deadline next week team",        # ham
    "click free offer limited time prize",   # spam
    "quarterly report attached review",      # ham
]
labels = [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]  # 1=spam, 0=ham

# Vectorize text
vectorizer = CountVectorizer()
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(emails)

# Train Naive Bayes (internally uses Bayes Theorem!)
clf = MultinomialNB()
clf.fit(X, labels)

# Predict
test_emails = ["free prize click win money", "team meeting agenda next week"]
X_test = vectorizer.transform(test_emails)
predictions = clf.predict(X_test)
probabilities = clf.predict_proba(X_test)

for email, pred, prob in zip(test_emails, predictions, probabilities):
    label = "SPAM" if pred == 1 else "HAM"
    print(f"Email: '{email[:30]}...'")
    print(f"  Prediction: {label} | Spam prob: {prob[1]:.2%}\n")
📤 Output
P(FREE): 0.275 P(Spam | FREE word): 0.873 = 87.3% Email: 'free prize click win money...' Prediction: SPAM | Spam prob: 99.98% Email: 'team meeting agenda next week...' Prediction: HAM | Spam prob: 0.12%
2.9 Hypothesis Testing — Statistical Decision Making
Hypothesis Testing kya hai?

Data ke basis pe statistical decisions lena. Kya ek AI model dusre se genuinely better hai, ya sirf luck? Hypothesis testing batata hai. Null Hypothesis (H₀) = "koi difference nahi" aur Alternative Hypothesis (H₁) = "difference hai".

ConceptAI ContextMeaning
p-valueA/B testing models≤0.05 = statistically significant
Null HypothesisModel A == Model BNo difference claim
Type I ErrorFalse PositiveH₀ reject kiya but true tha
Type II ErrorFalse NegativeH₀ accept kiya but false tha
Confidence IntervalModel accuracy range95% CI: [0.82, 0.89]
Statistical PowerTest sensitivityTrue effect detect karne ki ability
Python — A/B Testing for AI Models
from scipy import stats
import numpy as np

# ===== A/B Test: Model A vs Model B =====
# Model accuracy scores on 30 test batches
np.random.seed(42)
model_a = np.random.normal(loc=0.85, scale=0.05, size=30)
model_b = np.random.normal(loc=0.88, scale=0.05, size=30)

print(f"Model A — Mean: {model_a.mean():.4f}, Std: {model_a.std():.4f}")
print(f"Model B — Mean: {model_b.mean():.4f}, Std: {model_b.std():.4f}")

# T-test: Kya difference significant hai?
t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(model_a, model_b)

print(f"\nT-statistic: {t_stat:.4f}")
print(f"P-value:     {p_value:.4f}")

if p_value < 0.05:
    print("✅ Statistically significant! Model B genuinely better hai.")
else:
    print("❌ Not significant. Difference luck ho sakta hai.")

# ===== Confidence Interval =====
ci = stats.t.interval(
    confidence=0.95,
    df=len(model_b) - 1,
    loc=np.mean(model_b),
    scale=stats.sem(model_b)
)
print(f"\nModel B 95% CI: [{ci[0]:.4f}, {ci[1]:.4f}]")
# "95% sure hai ki Model B ka true accuracy is range mein hai"
2.10 Probability in LLMs — Temperature, Top-k, Top-p

Ye section practically sabse important hai AI engineers ke liye. Jab LLM text generate karta hai, woh ek probability distribution se sample karta hai.

LLM Token Sampling — Probability Distribution
📌 Remember This — LLM Parameters
Temperature (T): Probability distribution ko sharp ya flat karta hai.
T→0: Greedy (always highest prob) | T=1: Normal | T>1: Very creative/random

Top-k: Sirf top k tokens ko consider karo. k=1 = greedy decoding
Top-p (Nucleus sampling): Cumulative probability ≥ p tak tokens consider karo

ChatGPT default: Temperature ≈ 0.7-1.0, Top-p ≈ 0.95

🎯 Probability & Stats Interview Questions

Q1. "Bayes theorem explain karo aur batao ki LLMs mein probabilistic thinking kaise use hoti hai?"
Q2. "Temperature parameter kya karta hai? T=0 aur T=2 mein kya difference hai? Kab use karein?"
Q3. "Top-k aur Top-p sampling mein difference kya hai? Dono ka kab use karein?"
Q4. "A/B testing kaise karte hain do AI models ke beech? P-value kya hona chahiye?"
Q5. "Normal distribution AI mein kahan use hoti hai? Why do we initialize neural network weights with normal distribution?"

🖊 Probability & Statistics Exercises

  1. Apne 10 subjects ke marks ka mean, median, mode, variance, aur std deviation calculate karo Python mein.
  2. Bayes theorem use karke calculate karo: Agar ek disease 5% population mein hai aur test 90% accurate hai, toh positive test pe actually beemar hone ki probability kya hai?
  3. Normal distribution se 1000 random weights generate karo (μ=0, σ=0.1) aur histogram plot karo.
  4. Temperature 0.1, 0.7, aur 2.0 pe same probability distribution apply karo aur difference visualize karo.
  5. Ek coin 100 baar flip karo (Python random), heads aur tails count karo, aur chi-square test se check karo ki coin fair hai ya biased.

📌 Probability & Statistics — Key Takeaways

  • Mean, Median, Mode: Central tendency — data ka "typical" value
  • Variance & Std Dev: Spread — data kitna scattered hai
  • Normal Distribution: Bell curve — AI mein weight init, batch norm
  • Conditional Probability: Evidence ke baad update hoti probability
  • Bayes Theorem: Core of spam filters, Naive Bayes, probabilistic AI
  • Temperature: LLM output ka randomness control karta hai
  • Top-k / Top-p: Next token sampling strategies
  • Hypothesis Testing: Statistical validation of model improvements
CHAPTER 03 • PART 2

∂ Calculus Basics

Neural network training ka engine — gradients aur optimization

🌟Introduction — Calculus Kyun?

Neural network training ka poora process ek word mein describe ho sakta hai: optimization. Hum chahte hain ki model ka loss (error) minimize ho. Lekin kitne millions of parameters hain! Inhe kaise adjust karein? Answer hai: Gradient Descent, aur iske liye hume derivatives aur gradients samajhne padenge.

Analogy — Pahad Se Utrna (Blind Descent)
Socho tum ek pahaad ke upar ho, andhera hai, kuch dikhai nahi deta. Tumhe valley (lowest point) tak pahunchna hai — jo loss = minimum hai. Tum kya karoge?

Har step pe apne paon se mahsoos karo — kaunsi side steep hai (gradient). Phir usi direction mein ek chota step lo. Repeat. Yahi hai Gradient Descent! Derivative batata hai — "is jagah pe function kitni tezi se change ho raha hai" — aur hum usi information use karke weights update karte hain.
3.1 Derivatives — Instantaneous Rate of Change
Derivative kya hai?

Derivative batata hai ki ek function kitni tezi se change ho raha hai ek particular point pe. Isko slope bhi keh sakte hain — curve ke kisi bhi point pe tangent line ka slope.

Derivative — Basic Formula
f'(x) = df/dx = lim(h→0) [f(x+h) - f(x)] / h
Common derivatives:
d/dx (xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹  |  d/dx (eˣ) = eˣ  |  d/dx (ln x) = 1/x
d/dx (sin x) = cos x  |  d/dx (constant) = 0
Derivative — Slope at Different Points
💡 Beginner Intuition
Derivative basically ek question ka answer deta hai: "Agar main x mein thoda sa badhaunga, toh y mein kitna badhega?"
• Derivative positive → x badhane se y bhi badhega
• Derivative negative → x badhane se y ghattega
• Derivative zero → hum minimum ya maximum pe hain!
Neural network mein: derivative bolta hai "is weight ko badhane se loss badhega ya ghattega" — isliye hum opposite direction mein jaate hain.
3.2 Gradients — Multi-Variable Derivative
Gradient kya hai?

Jab function mein ek se zyada variables hain (jaise neural network ke lakho weights), toh gradient ek vector hota hai jo har variable ke partial derivative ko hold karta hai. Gradient batata hai — "highest increase ki direction kya hai".

Gradient of f(x, y)
∇f = [∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y]
∇ (nabla) = gradient | ∂f/∂x = partial derivative w.r.t x (y fixed)
Gradient Vector points in direction of steepest ascent
Gradient Descent: Gradient ki opposite direction mein move karo (minimize karne ke liye)

w = w - α × ∇L(w)   (weight update rule)
3.3 Partial Derivatives — Ek Time pe Ek Variable
Partial Derivative kya hai?

Jab multiple variables hain, toh ek ek karke derivative lete hain — baaki sab ko constant maanke. Neural network mein har weight ke liye alag partial derivative calculate hoti hai.

Partial Derivative Example
f(x, y) = 3x² + 2xy + y³
∂f/∂x = 6x + 2y    (y ko constant maano)
∂f/∂y = 2x + 3y²    (x ko constant maano)
3.4 Chain Rule — Backpropagation ka Heart
Chain Rule kya hai?

Jab functions ek doosre ke andar hoon (composite functions), toh derivative nikalne ka rule. Neural networks mein Backpropagation poori tarah chain rule use karta hai — output se input tak gradients compute karne ke liye.

Chain Rule
dy/dx = (dy/du) × (du/dx)
Agar y = f(u) aur u = g(x), toh dy/dx = f'(u) × g'(x)

Neural Network example:
Loss = L(output), output = σ(Wx + b)
∂L/∂W = (∂L/∂output) × (∂output/∂W) ← Chain Rule!
Backpropagation — Chain Rule in Neural Networks
3.5 Gradient Descent — Optimization ka Algorithm
Gradient Descent kya hai?

Loss function ko minimize karne ka iterative algorithm. Har iteration mein weights ko gradient ki opposite direction mein update karo — "downhill" jaao loss landscape mein.

Weight Update Rule
w_new = w_old − α × ∂L/∂w
w = weight parameter | α (alpha) = learning rate (step size)
∂L/∂w = gradient of loss w.r.t weight
Minus sign kyun? Gradient steep ascent direction hai — hum descent chahte hain!
TypeBatch SizeProsConsUse Case
Batch GDFull datasetStable, accurate gradientSlow, memory hungrySmall datasets
Stochastic GD1 sampleFast, noisy updates help escape local minimaNoisy, unstableOnline learning
Mini-batch GD32-512Balance of speed & stabilityHyperparameter tuningMost practical (default)
AdamMini-batchAdaptive, fast convergenceMemory overheadAlmost always! (default)
Python — Gradient Descent from Scratch
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ===== Manual Gradient Descent =====
# Problem: f(x) = x^2 + 4x + 4 ko minimize karo
# f'(x) = 2x + 4 (derivative)
# Minimum at x = -2 (f'(x) = 0 → 2x + 4 = 0 → x = -2)

def f(x):
    return x**2 + 4*x + 4

def gradient_f(x):
    return 2*x + 4   # Derivative of f(x)

# Gradient Descent
x = 10.0              # Starting point (random)
learning_rate = 0.1   # Step size
history = [x]

print("Gradient Descent Steps:")
print(f"{'Step':>4} | {'x':>8} | {'f(x)':>10} | {'gradient':>10}")
print("-" * 40)

for step in range(20):
    grad = gradient_f(x)         # Gradient calculate karo
    x = x - learning_rate * grad  # Weight update
    history.append(x)
    if step % 4 == 0:
        print(f"{step:>4} | {x:>8.4f} | {f(x):>10.4f} | {grad:>10.4f}")

print(f"\n🎯 Final x = {x:.4f} (expected: -2.0)")
print(f"🎯 Final f(x) = {f(x):.6f} (expected: 0.0)")

# ===== PyTorch Autograd (Real AI use) =====
import torch

# PyTorch automatically computes gradients!
x = torch.tensor(10.0, requires_grad=True)  # Track gradient

for i in range(20):
    loss = x**2 + 4*x + 4   # Forward pass
    loss.backward()           # Autograd: compute gradients
    with torch.no_grad():
        x -= 0.1 * x.grad      # Update
        x.grad.zero_()          # Zero gradient for next iteration

print(f"\nPyTorch result: x = {x.item():.4f}")
📤 Output
Gradient Descent Steps: Step | x | f(x) | gradient ---------------------------------------- 0 | 8.4000 | 82.2400 | 24.0000 4 | 3.2378 | 17.6000 | 14.4757 8 | 0.1424 | 4.6094 | 8.2849 12 | -1.1767 | 0.669 | 4.6466 16 | -1.7710 | 0.052 | 0.5581 🎯 Final x = -1.9815 (expected: -2.0) 🎯 Final f(x) = 0.000036 (expected: 0.0) PyTorch result: x = -2.0000
3.6 Activation Functions — Calculus in Action

Activation functions neural networks mein non-linearity add karte hain. Yeh functions aur unke derivatives directly gradient descent mein use hote hain backpropagation ke waqt.

ActivationFormulaDerivativeRangeUse Case
Sigmoidσ(x) = 1/(1+e⁻ˣ)σ(x)(1-σ(x))(0, 1)Binary classification output
ReLUmax(0, x)0 if x≤0, 1 if x>0[0, ∞)Hidden layers (most common)
Tanh(eˣ-e⁻ˣ)/(eˣ+e⁻ˣ)1 - tanh²(x)(-1, 1)RNNs, centered output
Softmaxeˣⁱ / ΣeˣʲComplex (Jacobian)(0, 1) sum=1Multi-class output, LLMs
GELUx·Φ(x)Smooth approximation≈(-∞, ∞)Transformers, GPT
SiLU/Swishx·σ(x)σ(x)+x·σ(x)(1-σ(x))≈(-∞, ∞)LLaMA, modern LLMs
Python — Activation Functions & Their Derivatives
import numpy as np

# ===== Activation Functions =====
def sigmoid(x):
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x))

def sigmoid_derivative(x):
    s = sigmoid(x)
    return s * (1 - s)   # Backprop mein use hota hai

def relu(x):
    return np.maximum(0, x)

def relu_derivative(x):
    return (x > 0).astype(float)  # 1 if positive, 0 if negative

def softmax(x):
    e_x = np.exp(x - np.max(x))  # Numerical stability
    return e_x / e_x.sum()

# Test values
z = np.array([-2, -1, 0, 1, 2], dtype=float)

print("z        :", z)
print("sigmoid  :", sigmoid(z).round(3))
print("sig'(z)  :", sigmoid_derivative(z).round(3))
print("relu     :", relu(z))
print("relu'(z) :", relu_derivative(z))

# LLM output — next token probabilities
logits = np.array([2.5, 1.2, 0.8, 3.1, 0.4])  # Raw scores
probs = softmax(logits)
print(f"\nLogits:       {logits}")
print(f"Probabilities: {probs.round(3)}")
print(f"Sum: {probs.sum():.1f}")  # Always 1.0
print(f"Predicted token: {np.argmax(probs)}")  # Highest prob token
📤 Output
z : [-2. -1. 0. 1. 2.] sigmoid : [0.119 0.269 0.5 0.731 0.881] sig'(z) : [0.105 0.197 0.25 0.197 0.105] ← Vanishing gradient! relu : [0. 0. 0. 1. 2.] relu'(z) : [0. 0. 0. 1. 1.] ← Clean gradient Logits: [2.5 1.2 0.8 3.1 0.4] Probabilities: [0.197 0.051 0.034 0.668 0.023] → Ek dominant token ki zyada probability Sum: 1.0 Predicted token: 3
3.7 Optimization Intuition — Loss Landscape
Loss Landscape — Local Minima, Global Minima, Saddle Points
⚠️ Common Mistakes
Vanishing Gradient: Deep networks mein sigmoid/tanh use karne se gradients bahut chhote ho jaate hain back layers tak — training ruk jaati hai. Solution: ReLU/GELU use karo, batch normalization karo.

Exploding Gradient: Gradients bohot bade ho jaate hain — NaN/Inf values. Solution: Gradient clipping use karo.

Learning Rate Too High: Loss oscillate karta hai, converge nahi hota.
Learning Rate Too Low: Training bohot slow — local minima mein phase jaata hai.

🎯 Calculus Interview Questions

Q1. "Backpropagation kya hai aur chain rule kaise use hoti hai isme? Simple example se explain karo."
Q2. "Vanishing gradient problem kya hai? Kaise solve karte hain? ReLU kyun better hai sigmoid se?"
Q3. "Learning rate ka kya role hai? Too high aur too low learning rate mein kya hota hai?"
Q4. "Gradient Descent, SGD, Mini-batch GD, aur Adam mein kya differences hain?"
Q5. "Softmax function kya karta hai aur yeh LLMs mein kahan use hota hai? Temperature ka softmax pe kya effect hai?"

🖊 Calculus Practice Exercises

  1. Manually gradient descent implement karo f(x) = (x-3)² pe. x=10 se start karo, lr=0.1 aur 30 iterations run karo.
  2. PyTorch mein ek simple linear regression model banao: y = 2x + 1. Random data generate karo aur model train karo MSE loss aur SGD optimizer se.
  3. Sigmoid, ReLU, aur Tanh functions ko -5 se +5 range mein plot karo aur unke derivatives bhi plot karo. Compare karo.
  4. NumPy se chain rule manually implement karo: f(x) = (3x + 1)² ke liye df/dx calculate karo x=2 pe.
  5. Softmax apply karo [1.0, 2.0, 3.0] pe temperature T=0.5 aur T=2.0 ke saath. Difference observe karo.

📌 Calculus — Key Takeaways

  • Derivative: Function kitna change hota hai — slope at a point
  • Gradient: Multi-variable derivative — neural network ka core
  • Partial Derivative: Ek variable ko vary karo, baaki constant
  • Chain Rule: Backpropagation ka mathematical foundation
  • Gradient Descent: Loss minimize karna — weights update karna
  • Learning Rate: Step size — too high = unstable, too low = slow
  • ReLU: Best activation for hidden layers (no vanishing gradient)
  • Softmax: Multi-class output probabilities — LLM token prediction
📖Glossary — Key Terms Reference
Scalar Ek single number. Koi direction nahi. e.g. learning_rate = 0.001
Vector Numbers ki ordered list. Direction + magnitude. e.g. word embedding [0.2, -0.5, 0.8]
Matrix 2D grid of numbers. Rows × Columns. e.g. weight matrix (784, 256)
Tensor N-dimensional array. PyTorch ka base. e.g. image batch (32, 3, 224, 224)
Dot Product Do vectors ka element-wise multiply + sum. Similarity measure.
Cosine Similarity Normalized dot product. -1 to +1 range. RAG systems mein use hota hai.
Eigenvalue/vector Av = λv. PCA, dimensionality reduction mein use. Data ka principal direction.
Mean (μ) Average. Σxᵢ/N. Central tendency ka measure.
Variance (σ²) Spread of data. Σ(xᵢ-μ)²/N. High variance = scattered data.
Standard Deviation (σ) √variance. Same units as data. More interpretable than variance.
Normal Distribution Bell curve. μ±σ mein 68%, μ±2σ mein 95% data hota hai.
Bayes' Theorem P(A|B) = P(B|A)×P(A)/P(B). Evidence se beliefs update karo.
Conditional Probability P(A|B) — B hone ke baad A ki probability.
P-value Statistical significance. <0.05 → result not due to chance.
Temperature (LLM) Output ka randomness. T=0 → greedy. T>1 → creative.
Softmax Raw scores → probabilities (sum=1). LLM output layer.
Derivative Rate of change. Slope at a point. df/dx. Neural net training ka core.
Gradient Multi-variable derivative vector. Points in direction of steepest ascent.
Partial Derivative ∂f/∂x — ek variable ka derivative, baaki constant.
Chain Rule d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x))·g'(x). Backpropagation ka foundation.
Gradient Descent w = w - α∇L(w). Loss minimize karne ka algorithm.
Learning Rate (α) Gradient descent ka step size. Too high = unstable. Too low = slow.
Backpropagation Chain rule use karke output se input tak gradients propagate karna.
ReLU max(0,x). Best hidden layer activation. No vanishing gradient.
🧠 Revision — Part 2 Mathematics for AI
Linear Algebra: Vectors = word embeddings | Matrices = weights, images | Matrix Mult = forward pass | Cosine sim = RAG retrieval

Probability & Stats: Mean/Std = data understanding | Normal dist = weight init | Bayes = spam filter, probabilistic AI | Temperature/Top-k/Top-p = LLM sampling

Calculus: Derivative = slope | Gradient = multi-variable slope | Chain rule = backpropagation | Gradient descent = training | ReLU = no vanishing gradient
Mathematics → AI Connection Map

Part 3 — Traditional AI & Machine Learning: In formulas ko actual algorithms mein apply karna — Linear Regression, Decision Trees, SVM, K-Means sab isi math pe based hain.
Part 4 — Deep Learning: Neural networks aur backpropagation in detail — ye sab directly is chapter se connect hote hain.
